speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five, interview with Regina Mauskopf. You had just described an incident--

speaker: interviewee
text: The beating.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where you were beaten. What other incidents do you recall, that comes to mind, similar to that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the rest of it was going-- as they say, when I was [? riding that ?] [NON-ENGLISH] in the hospital. They call it the [NON-ENGLISH]. And so I asked the nurse there-- I didn't see a doctor there, but-- and she was in white, dressed, Jewish, naturally. And I told her, my sisters are there. And they're talking that they're gonna-- we're gonna be evacuated here, from Plaszow. And I would like to be together with [? the things. ?] Well, she said nothing at first. But then, she agreed, and she signed me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she signed me out. I didn't go to work, because they had to assign me, first. And I couldn't be too much outside, either. [? In here, ?] is they already are talking. They just don't know when. Well, anyway, the-- the time came when they gave an order that we gonna go from there. What am I doing? Now, I'm here. And, you know, we gonna be separated now, because they're going to put us, maybe, different trains, and different whatever. So I went back again, to my blockalteste because that woman was changed with her daughter. The daughter was in my block. So she said, just tell from Mrs. So-and-so, that she should come here. And you go to your nieces. It's easier, because I'm just one person to go to three of them, and she comes to the daughter. And that's what we did.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then we were assigned-- it was already evening-- to another barrack, kind of, where it's right by the trains, there-- a place. And-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and then she start calling the names. I was still sick. That's right, it was right after [INAUDIBLE] moved in, and we went to the-- to Auschwitz the second time, because I was still not feeling so good, after that [INAUDIBLE]. And when she was calling the names there, you know, that she should have there, for the train-- but I don't remember if she called by the number-- by the name, because that's how she found out.

speaker: interviewer
text: You didn't have a number, then, did you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, not on our arms, no-- not a tattoo. And-- and then I answered Mrs.-- she was do something later, like Rosenberg, something that woman. She was not far from the third village, with her-- with her daughter. And I answered on her name. And she said, what are you doing? Instead her name.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said to her, do you remember when I asked you, once, that I want to change with Mrs. So-and-so, because I had my sisters here, and she had her daughter there? She didn't listen, nothing, and she started the same thing over my face, like the other guy. She hit you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she was a Jewish--

speaker: interviewee
text: Jewish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Elster?

speaker: interviewee
text: The blockalteste.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alteste.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And that's it. She let me stay. She made over the name. I don't know what happened with the other one. I don't even remember, when I got home, if they came home or not. I really don't remember. And-- so we came back to Auschwitz, and back to the staying there. It was evening when we got there, and everybody started to get undressed right outside.

speaker: interviewer
text: Before you continue, how long, all together, would you say were you in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: From the-- like the 15th of May, the first time, to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewee
text: '44. Three days later, we went to Plaszow. And then, after Tisha B'Av. Like a week later, something like that, maybe two-- then when they packed us back into Auschwitz, back.

speaker: interviewer
text: So all together, about two weeks you were in Plaszow?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was more.

speaker: interviewer
text: More than that?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was more. Yeah, it was-- when is Tisha B'Av, in August?

speaker: interviewer
text: Something like that.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, and we went in May. End of May, we came there.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you keep track of the Jewish holidays?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was always somebody. There was always somebody that knew. People had [INAUDIBLE], sometimes. We've been with, in [INAUDIBLE]. That's how he knew about the-- Well, we-- we counted the week. Like Tisha B'Av and-- so approximately. Even if it was not the same day-- I [INAUDIBLE] Hanukkah a few years-- we started the first Hanukkah with [INAUDIBLE]. The next day, I found out, forgot to look at [INAUDIBLE]. Hanukkah, you know, it was-- I think it was in the store. You know? So that's-- Well, anyway.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so you arrived back to Auschwitz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Back to Auschwitz, yeah, back again to the-- the lousing thing there. And then we got kind of a-- it was, everybody got the same uniform, like-- like it was gray dresses. It was just like a nightgown with short sleeves. And that's it. That's what we got. Somebody, if there was a kind of bunch of [? ruffles ?] or whatever, you could make a little belt, if there was enough. Everybody grabbed, if-- if there was. And who didn't had, didn't had. That's how we got the clothes. And then we went into barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you get a number?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, the number came like in a few weeks later. It came.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that was tattooed?

speaker: interviewee
text: And yeah, then when-- yeah. That was the tattoo on my--

speaker: interviewer
text: And what is your number?

speaker: interviewee
text: My number is-- I have to be looking at it. It's A-19466. And so we've been there, and got-- stayed there in-- in [INAUDIBLE] there, too. We were kind of like cramped. But we could sleep, somehow.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember what-- which block, or which--

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. It had a three in it, but I don't know. Was it 31, or-- or 13? Something like that. I don't know. It was in the middle of the-- not far away from where-- from the gate.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many sleeping places, in-- in the bunk that you were in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, there was a long one, a long barrack. But they were [INAUDIBLE]. And it had, in the middle, kind of like a chimney. We always asked, what for that thing, if they make fire in there. But there was never fire in it. So we went there, and sometimes-- Yeah, there was another thing. We wound up in another barrack before we went to Guben. First of all, in the same barrack, the [INAUDIBLE], that means that-- what she took care on-- on the barrack-- when, you know, in the [INAUDIBLE], and kept on announcing that if there's girls 14 or younger, they should all come and stay out, not be in the-- in the [INAUDIBLE], the, you know, the bed. And well, we knew, already, that time approximately, that-- what happens to young people, because in-- in Plaszow, they were-- we saw a bunch of children. And the Germans dressed them up like little German things. There was about 10 of them, something. I don't know. And they were marching in exactly the way that [INAUDIBLE] was, with them. And they were going out there. And we afterwards, we asked somebody-- because we-- we saw them a few times, and then all of a sudden they're not there. So I asked one of those older people-- which are not older people, but they were before us, there. And she said, you know where they are. That's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we were kind of ready, a little bit, with knowledge that what-- what's going on. And so [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] there was another thing, before, when we came to Auschwitz the second time. We were staying outside, naked-- outside, and we were very thirsty. And again, and so somehow the Germans, they went and got a barrel-- a big, big barrel. And then people stood, I don't know, there. And everybody got-- there was a big pile of pots, kind of, cups. And everybody went in and had water.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I gathered my cup, I don't know what happened. The cup drowned, fell out. So I stood, already, in this side, between those people which had water, already. The girls kept on saying to me, my nieces, said Regina, switch in, switch in. You see, switch in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I did, and I switched in. As long as my water food were not yet there, in that line for the water, I felt a hand over my neck. And I fell to the ground. And I was naked. Well, it was that gravel-- sharp gravel. I got this side, whole was scratched and bloody. So anyway, that was finished and then we were in the barracks. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got lost, about-- about the thing, there. I have to leave that, now, and to tell what happened when my nieces went, and when they came, that the 14-year-olds should stay out. So as the-- from one side, she's supposed to come in-- the SS woman, to-- whatever they would have done, with the 14-year-olds. I went in the front, at that gate, and that door, and started begging the other girl-- she was a Jewish. She said-- you know, I said, I have her here, and let me out. And somehow, maybe, I'll switch her in-- something. And she said, she can't do it. She said, then she say, all right. You want to be shot? Go ahead. And she let me out.

speaker: interviewee
text: She let me out, through there. And I went by the wall, kind of, you know-- her, by the hand, and kind of-- we was kind of slowly, till we got back there. So the SS woman went out this way. I don't know if she got the other girls, 14 years old, or not. And I came back the other way, there. Could have been, there was-- I don't know. He was preoccupied with something, the-- the guy. He was not thinking, because nobody was walking around outside. It was not allowed. So he was sure that nobody is outside.

speaker: interviewee
text: So that was one. And then right away-- and I was still kind of that scratch, I think. And we were not there long, because for Rosh Hashanah, we were already in Guben. And we came after the Tisha B'Av. So we were not long there. And in the meantime, we the tattoo, and that little 14-year-old was fainting, there. [INAUDIBLE] there was mud, water. And we were putting the wet mud around, I think, on her face to revive her. And we have like this. Daughter has the higher-- highest number. She went first. Then I went, and then-- like, you know, we have all the three and the four of us, like just a number. The numbers are in sequence.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. So anyway, so then all of a sudden came a-- she was in civilian dressed, a heavyset lady-- and start looking us over, from our barrack. I don't know if she looked over from other barracks too, or not. But start looking us over, our body.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she picked some girls out of that. I don't know what they had, you know. And she took a look at my scratches. She put me in between them, too. We ask ourselves, why are they looking over this? [INAUDIBLE], you know that scabs, you know that disease? They don't have it here. Some skin disease?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, skin disease between the finger-- you know, it's scratchy. So we used to [INAUDIBLE]. And they went, and she took me there. Here I am, separated from them. I don't know what she is going to do with them. I kept on showing for my girls, and saying, kind of, scratch yourself. In Yiddish-- scratch yourself, scratch yourself. You know? Or maybe Hungarian, because they didn't talk. You know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they start, all the three of them, scratching themselves. You know. And they went with me. So we went not far away, in a barrack. And they were all there [INAUDIBLE]. The Pole-- the Polish singers there, the theater people were there. And so we've been there, I don't know, just a few days, maybe, or something like that. In Auschwitz, the second time?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Auschwitz, the second time, in that barrack-- in that sick barrack. Let's put it that way. And then, when the-- we had to get undressed again. [INAUDIBLE] the clothes were the same clothes on our hand. And there he sits, again, on the chair.

speaker: interviewer
text: Referring to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Mengele. And just showing us, like this. You know? We thought we are going to the crematorium, because the block-- the barrack is a sick barrack thing. And there were girls, which were going in a different sides, or back to the barracks. And we were out. And lucky for the four of us, we went through. And we went through. And I don't remember if we went again, for delousing, or not. And then, when they packed us in again, we didn't go already, even to that barrack-- just back to the train. And we went to Guben.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you never worked in Auschwitz, itself?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No, no.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the-- the-- the person in charge of the bunk or the barracks, do you remember her name?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it a woman?

speaker: interviewee
text: A woman, yeah, girls-- all girls. There, I want to say I remember that about a man's barracks. Right there, there was a man's barrack, too. And we saw-- I saw corpses there, outside-- you know, because in the evening, [? the whole thing. ?] And there, I saw one, as I said. I didn't see him running to the-- to the electricity, but he was just hanging there on the thing, and things. So we went to Guben, and--

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you get to Guben?

speaker: interviewee
text: By train, again.

speaker: interviewer
text: Again in cattle cars?

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And about how many in a cattle car?

speaker: interviewee
text: We were-- I think it was more pleasant. It was more pleasant. It was-- again, with the bucket in the corner. And we came there in the evening.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it took about a day or less?

speaker: interviewee
text: Something like that, because it's further than from the-- from Auschwitz to Plaszow, because that's like in the middle of Germany, almost. I didn't look how far it is, which way from Munich. But I did found it on the-- on the thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: Map?

speaker: interviewee
text: On the map.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did they tell you where they-- where they were taking you?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, never. No, never. So we came there. And there we found out that there is a munitions factory in-- in the city, not in the camp. And we came-- it was in the evening. And we thought, compared to other places, it looked like a sanatorium, because there was not too many women. And we all-- they all had that grey dresses, and everybody had a towel. And from the towel, they made a turban. You know, the woman looked so nice. You know, we were without hair, and the turban on the head, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was so quiet in there. The people which were there, already, they were kind of running around, and accepting us, and everything is shh, you know. And assigned to the barracks. And we had a mattress. We had bunk beds. And there was, already-- that-- from wood, what do you call it? You pack glass? Wood pieces?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wood shavings.

speaker: interviewer
text: Shavings.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, that long-- Yeah. That was-- was in the mattresses. So we had mattresses there, you know. So the-- the girls were sleeping in the bottom, and I bunked with my cousin, Dora. I say the girls, all the time.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was it like a camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's a camp with barracks, yeah. It was, with electricity around. Maybe there wasn't, but it looked like. You know, who would have dared?

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you know how many, approximately, how many women were there?

speaker: interviewee
text: When we came there, I don't know how many there was. We were 1,000 girls, with us, when we came there. And there was a kitchen-- not a big one. And-- and I was-- we worked together the first couple of nights, with my nieces. But then, they were assigned to the factory, to go to work. And they picked me out, because I was kind of-- they picked up strong girls for the working, in the camps, kind of the lager personnel, they called it. And so they started work in the thing. And some-- they were changing night shifts and day shifts, a week here, a week there-- you know, different. And my cousin and-- there were two girls, which we got acquainted in Auschwitz. They were Romanian, two sisters. And they were kind of two strong ones. And we kind of got teamed up there, with the work. And they called us the four horses. They always-- whatever, we did something else, then they gave us that name. And so we washed the barracks. I was specially assigned for the-- for the blockalteste. She was, too, from Slovakia and very fine. You know, the whole camp was very kind of-- this is like a working place. All right, we didn't have-- we had better food than all over. We had like, twice a week, like a [INAUDIBLE]. It was almost like a pudding, with macaroni in it, and it was sweet. It was really good.

speaker: interviewer
text: Any meat?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Vegetables?

speaker: interviewee
text: Ah, cabbage, cabbage. The first couple of weeks we got-- kind of-- I got kind of better acquainted, with the working place, there. And I volunteered, whatever it was, something just to get a little bit more soup-- save for them, for my nieces, and then I had-- if I ate, already, in the kitchen a little bit more, then I could save my portion, already, for them. So-- but in the beginning, as long as you got close-- not just me, in any camp-- if you got close to the trash, you were lucky, because you ate whatever you found, the pieces. I remembered over there, for-- from the cabbage, the pieces of leaves in the garbage. And we went and wiped [? it out, ?] [? the scent. ?] And we ate, till they assigned us to work, you know, the couple of days. But then, when we got assigned to there, and when they were night shift, and I used to go and help out in the kitchen, because I was finished with my work in the barracks-- to wash the floor and to see should be clean. Every barrack had two other girls. I was with my cousin, Dora. And they were those military, big things, what they cooked the potatoes in, in the-- in the peel-- called them the [INAUDIBLE]. So they called me in, to pick out from the-- you know, because they were late. The night-- the day shift-- the night shift has to go to work, and the day shift comes in. There is, you know, in that time they gave the-- the [? soups ?] out. So they called me in to help out, to take out those potatoes from-- from the things. And my nieces were sleeping, still, at that time.

speaker: interviewee
text: Do you know what I did? I was throwing potatoes in my bosom. I had a little [INAUDIBLE] in here, and throw [INAUDIBLE] potatoes were hot, you know? And I was not allowed to go to their barracks because they were sleeping. But I-- I took a lot of risk, all the time.

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- and I put, you know, it in, in there, whatever there was. I had a little apron, which I didn't need it, kind of, to use it. I wrapped those potatoes, and put for them to their feet. It warmed them while they were sleeping, and it warmed them when they got up. And on top of that, they were those cans-- you know, from-- from some certain thing in the kitchen. I made two holes, with a piece of string. And while, as I say, I had been kind of close to the kitchen, so I ate more than other ones. So my soup, I could give them. They went to work, and they had what to eat there. That's what she writes. I wish you could read that Hungarian letter.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so things like that, it was there. And another thing-- like, I had my shoes. And the little one, I don't remembered. They fell apart, the shoes. And she was wearing these Holland shoes-- you know, the wooden ones? Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the snow, you know. It was winter time, kind of got clogged up on that wood. And you know, and you could, always, even break a leg, you know, because it was going this and that way. So I didn't like this, [INAUDIBLE] when she was working night shift. I gave-- she was wearing my combat boots. And I took her wooden shoes for the night, because I did not need them. That's [INAUDIBLE] shows the two-- two things for me. So and then--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were called a potato lady?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. This is when the-- the war was starting to come close up. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: But that was later, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: But that was already before, you know, in-- in February. In February when the-- the front was kind of-- I don't know who was going to Ravensbruck, the Russians or the Americans. So they-- from Ravensbruck-- from Ravensbruck, you know, they came a lot of girls, from there, and probably from other things, where we didn't have the-- to accommodate them, the barracks. They were outside. They came frozen, because they marched, too-- the death march.

speaker: interviewer
text: They came to Guben?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Guben. Yeah. And there they were hanging-- at that time, I was not washing, already, the barracks. They took me into the kitchen, already, to help out, because they had to feed more, you know, whatever they were-- a little bit of some more soups.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I wasn't-- they assigned me to bring in-- you know, there was like, almost like this room, but full of potatoes-- to bring in with baskets, from the kitchen, to put them into that things. I didn't finish the bag of potatoes, was what happened to me. Once I exactly put into those things, and I wanted to say, for [INAUDIBLE], that I had to go to the latrine. Otherwise, you know, that's how I went out with the hot potatoes. And exactly as I, kind of think, should I put another one or not? And I hear they say, [GERMAN]. And about three or four officers came in. And they stopped in the front of me, because as I wanted to go with those potatoes, I always picked the place by the door. You know? And there I was standing. They were burning. I had blisters from the hot potatoes, there. I thought, if they gonna find out that they are there, they'll shoot me. And in Plaszow, they almost-- that time, I thought, I'll be shot. So can I go back? Sure.

speaker: interviewee
text: As I was working in the-- between those military clothes, and they wore turtleneck sweaters, the gray one-- military. So I got for myself, I got for the girls, certain things [INAUDIBLE] we knitted [? over things, ?] whatever, you know. And when we were going away, I went and I had a little shirt, small, with short sleeves. And there was a girl, that she didn't have enough clothes for her. I said, you put that on, whatever you want for panties or whatever, because just to [INAUDIBLE]. And-- but in case, if I lose my thing, if they going to see me, that then they-- who knows? Then you're gonna have to give me that back. And sure enough, when they packed us in, in the cattle cars, first underneath the skirt, I tied, again, had a little bottle with water-- again, was hanging from me, underneath the skirt. And we were [INAUDIBLE] there. People were fainting. So that little bottle of water went 1, 2, 3, away. Because, you know, everybody, you know, from the things. But then, all of a sudden, there is a knock. It was already sealed, the door. All of a sudden, there is-- the door, you know. We hear the doors opening. And the German soldiers says, there is a woman in a military sweater. I want her right here, to the door.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I just came to the door, and I took off the sweater, and the top was naked. I thought they're going to take me out, and shoot me. But they didn't. And I went back in, and the girl gave me back that little polo shirt. And that's it. This is the end of tape number five.
